picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the irreplaceable center of the AI infrastructure buildout: $571B in AI capex projected for 2026 flows disproportionately through NVIDIA's H100/H200/Blackwell stack, with ~75%+ gross margins and the highest ROIC in semis. Today's tech correction (-11% from June 2 peak) offers a better entry as the stock pulls from its $236 52-week high toward the $200–206 range, which is well above the $141 52-week low. Consensus forecasts 46.3% annual EPS growth and estimates continue to be revised upward; Zacks Rank #2 with analyst consensus Strong Buy. The AI capex supercycle — which Goldman expects to account for roughly half of S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026 — keeps NVDA at the top of the growth book.
picks.catalyst: Blackwell GPU ramp confirmation and Q2 FY2027 earnings with data center revenue guidance — next hyperscaler capex announcements (MSFT, AMZN, GOOGL all scheduled) will re-rate AI infrastructure names
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$205, down ~13% from $236.54 52-week high; forward P/E ~25x — elevated but more digestible after the tech correction; consolidating near 50-day moving average support post-CPI selloff; current range is the best entry in 3 months
picks.exitTrigger: Sequential gross margin compression below 72%, or two consecutive quarters of hyperscaler capex guidance cuts signaling AI infrastructure pause; loss of major design wins to AMD MI400 or Google TPUs
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export control tightening on H100/H200 chips to China and Middle East markets cutting a material revenue stream

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet is the single most attractively valued mega-cap AI name at ~22x forward P/E — a material discount to NVDA, MSFT, and AMZN — while generating ~55%+ gross margins, exceptional FCF, and one of the highest ROICs in the S&P 500. Berkshire/Abel tripled the position in Q1 2026 to ~57.8M shares worth ~$16.6B, the most visible institutional endorsement of the quarter. Google Cloud reacceleration, Gemini AI monetization across Search and YouTube, and the proprietary TPU hardware stack (deployed internally for a decade) make GOOGL the best risk-adjusted large-cap growth name in the universe. Today's pullback to $348 from a $408 52-week high creates a compelling asymmetric entry.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) with Google Cloud revenue growth re-acceleration and Gemini search monetization metrics; Google-Samsung chip partnership announcement per Reuters reporting today
picks.entryNote: Trading at $348.19, down ~15% from $408.61 52-week high; forward P/E ~22x vs. 5-year average of ~24x; Berkshire added aggressively at higher prices, making current levels more attractive; stock is near YTD consolidation support
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerating below 25% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or material loss of Search market share to OpenAI/Perplexity showing up in comScore data; DOJ antitrust structural breakup order
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedy forcing divestiture of Chrome or Android, disrupting the Search advertising flywheel

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom occupies a unique position as the custom ASIC king — its XPU chips for Meta, Alphabet, and Apple provide diversified AI revenue without the export-control risk that overhangs NVDA. Druckenmiller added AVGO as a new position in Q1 2026, validating the AI hardware diversification thesis. Custom silicon (ASICs) represents a $50B+ TAM by 2027 as hyperscalers reduce NVDA dependency; Broadcom captures a disproportionate share of that shift. VMware software integration is driving margin expansion, with infrastructure software now ~40% of revenue mix. The stock has corrected from recent highs following the early June earnings report, offering improved entry.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report (expected September) with VMware cross-sell metrics and custom ASIC backlog update; any public announcement of a new XPU hyperscaler customer would be a significant re-rating event
picks.entryNote: Pulled back from peak after June earnings beat-but-disappoint on AI guidance; trading at a more reasonable entry vs. late May highs; forward P/E ~28x — premium but justified by VMware integration synergies and AI ASIC ramp; momentum in sector rotation screen confirms institutional accumulation on dips
picks.exitTrigger: VMware integration synergy targets miss by >15%, or hyperscaler custom ASIC wins fail to materialize in backlog disclosures; gross margin compression below 65% on software mix shift
picks.keyRisk: AVGO priced for perfection after multi-year re-rating; any guidance trim on AI ASIC timelines triggers outsized multiple compression given crowd positioning

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the convergence of the two most powerful themes in the portfolio: AI infrastructure (AWS is the #1 cloud provider and direct beneficiary of enterprise AI spending) and consumer e-commerce resilience as a value-oriented destination for the stressed consumer rotating away from discretionary. Tepper nearly doubled his AMZN stake to ~15.2% of Appaloosa's portfolio in Q1 2026, calling it a bet on AI capex converting into margin expansion. Ackman also moved AMZN to his #2 position. AWS AI inference and training workloads, combined with margin expansion from advertising and third-party logistics, make AMZN one of the strongest multi-year compounders in the universe. Consumer stress actually benefits Amazon's value proposition vs. traditional retail.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) with AWS revenue acceleration and operating margin expansion; any announcement of new AI inference pricing or Trainium2 chip adoption by enterprise customers
picks.entryNote: Held up relatively well vs. NVDA/AVGO in the tech correction given AWS defensive characteristics; forward P/E ~32x — elevated but AWS operating income mix justifies premium; Tepper added at higher prices, making current levels constructive; stock is in the sector rotation sweet spot (cloud + value retail dual thesis)
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerating below 20% YoY, or operating margin guidance cut below 8% on excessive AI capex investment with no monetization visibility; meaningful market share loss to Azure or GCP
picks.keyRisk: AWS margin compression from aggressive AI infrastructure capex investment before revenue recognition; regulatory antitrust action on AWS market dominance

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) is the stealth winner of the AI training workload buildout — its backlog has grown >100% YoY driven by AI-hungry hyperscalers and sovereign cloud deployments, with OCI capacity constraints being the primary bottleneck on growth. The company has posted 4 consecutive EPS beats, with analyst consensus price target at $273.08. Goldman Sachs research identifies ORCL alongside MSFT as a key name where AI revenue monetization is already showing up in billings data — the transition from capex promise to earnings reality is happening now. FCF yield of ~3% and gross margin expansion as higher-margin cloud mix increases make this a quality-growth compounder with improving estimate revision trajectory.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (expected mid-June) with OCI backlog and remaining performance obligation (RPO) disclosure — any RPO acceleration above $100B would be a major catalyst; sovereign cloud deals in Middle East and Europe are incremental near-term drivers
picks.entryNote: Consensus price target $273.08; trading at approximately 22x forward earnings — reasonable for a cloud compounder with 4-consecutive beat cadence; OCI capacity expansion announcements have been pulling forward demand; IGV's largest weighting at 9.65% confirms institutional positioning
picks.exitTrigger: OCI backlog growth decelerating below 50% YoY, or RPO fails to show sequential acceleration in Q4; any database migration acceleration to competing platforms (Snowflake, Databricks) reduces core moat
picks.keyRisk: Legacy database business secular decline accelerates faster than OCI cloud ramp, creating revenue air pocket; valuation re-rating risk if AI spending sentiment reverses broadly

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-conviction energy name in the portfolio — the cheapest sector on both absolute and relative 10-year P/E metrics, with the broadest positive earnings revision breadth in the S&P 500. WTI crude settled at $90.03/barrel Wednesday as Trump escalated Iran threats, providing a direct fundamental tailwind. With the Strait of Hormuz risk elevated and Morgan Stanley's base case at $90/barrel by year-end, XOM's upstream production mix and Pioneer integration synergies provide durable earnings power. Energy is the sector rotation scorecard co-leader as of June 11, and XOM is specifically cited as a direct beneficiary alongside Caterpillar and Walmart of the real-economy rotation. The 3–4% dividend yield adds income return during the holding period.
picks.catalyst: Continued Iran conflict escalation keeping WTI above $85/barrel; Q2 2026 earnings showing Pioneer synergy realization and Guyana production ramp; any Strait of Hormuz closure would push WTI materially higher
picks.entryNote: Energy sector up ~26-27% YTD; XOM trading near multi-year highs but at roughly 32% discount to 10-year average P/E — a rare combination of price momentum AND deep fundamental undervaluation; WTI at $90 supports current earnings run-rate; dividend yield ~3.5% provides downside cushion
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained WTI below $70/barrel for more than one quarter (Iran deal + demand destruction), or Pioneer synergy realization falls materially below $2B/year target; Guyana production plateau surprise
picks.keyRisk: Iran ceasefire / diplomatic resolution causing rapid oil price normalization toward $65-70/barrel, compressing margins and triggering multiple de-rating

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier intersection of the defense spending supercycle and AI-adjacent industrial re-rating — its Pratt & Whitney engines and Raytheon missile/defense systems are direct beneficiaries of both Middle East conflict escalation and NATO defense budget expansion post-Ukraine. The Iran war has made defense an immediate real-economy theme, not a 10-year forecast. Industrials received XLI total return of 24.19% over the past year, with defense and aerospace being the primary earnings drivers. RTX's backlog provides multi-year earnings visibility, and the company has returned to growth after the Pratt GTF engine inspection headwinds resolved. Elliott Management's global activist campaigns are targeting Industrials and Consumer Discretionary, suggesting sector-wide attention to capital allocation improvement.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July showing Pratt & Whitney revenue recovery and Raytheon defense segment backlog growth; any NATO/European defense spending increase announcement adds to backlog revaluation
picks.entryNote: Defense names have underperformed tech YTD in the rally but are showing strength on geopolitical escalation; RTX trading at ~17x forward EPS — historically fair for a defense compounder; Pratt GTF resolution removes the primary overhang that has kept the stock below pre-crisis levels; relative strength improving vs. S&P 500
picks.exitTrigger: Pratt & Whitney GTF inspection costs re-accelerate above $3B total charge guidance, or a surprise Iran ceasefire that simultaneously reduces defense spending urgency; Raytheon losing major missile contract re-competition
picks.keyRisk: Federal budget sequestration or DOGE-driven DoD spending cuts affecting Raytheon missile and electronic warfare programs, particularly if Iran conflict deescalates

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-quality expression of the capital markets reactivation thesis — M&A and IPO pipelines are rebuilding after a two-year drought, alternative asset management (Goldman Sachs Alternatives) is scaling, and NIM benefits from higher-for-longer rates support the lending book. Financials receive an Outperform rating for 2026 supported by robust bank profitability. XLF trades at ~14-15x forward P/E — among the cheapest sector multiples in the S&P 500. GS specifically benefits from private credit deal-making and the infrastructure financing boom tied to AI buildout and energy capex, which the Goldman Research arm has quantified as a multi-trillion dollar opportunity. The yield curve's positive 2s10s spread (+38 bps) is structurally supportive of banking NIM.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) showing M&A advisory revenue acceleration and FICC trading desk performance amid elevated volatility; any Fed hold confirmation (June 16-17 meeting) removes uncertainty overhang for financial stocks
picks.entryNote: Financials have rotated in as tech has sold off — exactly the breadth dynamic identified in the macro briefing; GS at ~14x forward P/E is historically cheap for a tier-1 investment bank; 2s10s spread widening is a direct NIM tailwind; insider buying patterns have been constructive
picks.exitTrigger: M&A deal volume contracts >20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or credit quality deterioration signals (HY spreads above 450 bps) indicating a credit cycle turn that hits GS's loan book
picks.keyRisk: A hawkish Fed surprise hiking rates aggressively flattens or re-inverts the yield curve, compressing NIM and freezing deal activity simultaneously

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global is a toll-booth compounder at the intersection of data infrastructure, financial intelligence, and fixed-income issuance — revenue streams that benefit from both AI-driven data demand (the Marketplace and Market Intelligence segments) and elevated corporate bond issuance in a higher-nominal-rate environment. As the dominant credit ratings duopoly (alongside Moody's), SPGI earns royalties on every corporate bond issuance, and the AI capex buildout is creating a massive wave of investment-grade debt financing. The IHS Markit merger synergies are now flowing through margins, and data analytics revenue is growing faster than ratings. High ROIC, near-70% gross margins, and consistent EPS beats make this a quality compounder for the 2-5 year horizon.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings showing Ratings revenue acceleration from energy-sector and AI-infrastructure bond issuance; Vitesse (Commodity Insights) segment benefiting from elevated energy price environment
picks.entryNote: Held near medium conviction vs. high for larger positions; trading at ~26x forward P/E — slight premium to 10-year average but justified by IHS Markit synergies and data segment re-rating; relative to GS provides sector diversification within Financials on different revenue drivers
picks.exitTrigger: Corporate bond issuance volume collapses as credit spreads widen above 150 bps (IG OAS), decimating ratings revenue; or a major regulatory action forcing SPGI to divest the Ratings business
picks.keyRisk: A deep credit crunch reducing both ratings volume and financial data subscription renewals simultaneously, hitting both revenue streams in the same downturn

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: Merck provides essential sector and factor diversification as a high-quality pharma with defensive cash flows, trading at a material discount to both the S&P 500 and its own 10-year median P/E — XLV's 19.94x forward P/E is already below index, and MRK trades at a further discount. Keytruda (pembrolizumab) remains the world's best-selling oncology drug with multi-year patent protection, generating ~$25B+ in annual revenue with significant biosimilar runway. The animal health (Organon spin-off benefit) and vaccine pipeline provide diversification. Healthcare is rated as a sector positioned for mean reversion and potential outperformance in 2026, and MRK is the quality anchor that provides income (dividend yield ~2.5%) and defensive characteristics in the late-cycle stagflationary regime without the Managed Care regulatory risk that haunts UNH/HUM.
picks.catalyst: Keytruda combination therapy Phase 3 readouts and FDA approvals in new oncology indications expanding TAM; any IRA drug pricing reform dilution by Congress reduces the primary overhang on pharma multiples
picks.entryNote: MRK has been a perennial holdover in the book — thesis remains intact as pharma defensive anchor; trading at ~13x forward P/E — deep discount to S&P 500's 25x; Keytruda biosimilar cliff is 2028+, providing 2-3 year earnings visibility window; dividend yield ~2.5% compensates for holding during macro uncertainty
picks.exitTrigger: Keytruda pricing cuts >20% via IRA negotiation, or a surprise Phase 3 failure in a key indication (non-small cell lung cancer combo) that contracts the TAM materially; FDA manufacturing compliance action
picks.keyRisk: IRA drug price negotiation targeting Keytruda sooner than 2028 expected timeline, creating a revenue overhang that compresses multiples and forces guidance cuts

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares
picks.rationale: GLD is the highest-conviction defensive position in the portfolio given the concurrent geopolitical (Iran war, Strait of Hormuz), monetary (real Fed funds rate near zero at +5 bps), and fiscal (record Treasury issuance) tailwinds for gold. Central bank buying remains well above long-run averages as dollar reserve diversification accelerates. With CPI at 4.2% and the real rate barely positive, the classic gold bull case — negative or zero real rates combined with geopolitical risk premium — is fully intact. Gold has pulled back from its $509 GLD 52-week high to the $370-$390 range, providing a better risk/reward entry for a macro hedge position.
picks.catalyst: Further Iran escalation after Trump's Wednesday threats ('attacking them very hard'); BOJ rate hike on June 16 triggering yen carry unwind and dollar weakness; any downside surprise in June jobs or growth data reigniting stagflation narrative
picks.entryNote: GLD pulled back significantly from $509 52-week high — the correction has reset positioning and provides a cleaner entry; real Fed funds rate near zero (+5 bps) is the most gold-supportive interest rate environment since 2020; central banks buying at above-average pace provides structural floor
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire leading to sustained oil price decline below $70, AND Fed delivers three or more rate hikes pushing real rates above 1.5% — only the combination of both would materially damage the gold thesis
picks.keyRisk: A surprise Iran peace deal combined with a significantly hawkish Fed pushing real rates sharply positive — dual shock that would pressure gold from both geopolitical and rate directions simultaneously

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is the cleanest risk-off diversifier in the defensive sleeve — it screens for quality factors (high ROE, FCF coverage, dividend growth consistency), naturally underweights high-P/E AI names, and generates a 3.5%+ yield that is meaningful when equities are range-bound. In the late-cycle stagflationary regime identified in the macro briefing, SCHD's factor profile (value + quality + income) provides genuine diversification from the AI-heavy mega-cap portfolio, particularly when Goldman's Risk Appetite Indicator sits at the 99th percentile of all observations since 1991. KO hitting all-time highs Wednesday while tech sold off is exactly the defensive rotation dynamic that makes SCHD relevant today.
picks.catalyst: Continued rotation into defensives and dividend stocks as tech corrects and the inflation-sensitive consumer shows stress; any Fed hold/hike confirmation at June 16-17 meeting accelerates dividend stock appeal as real income alternatives
picks.catalystWindow: ongoing — particularly acute next 4-6 weeks into the Fed meeting
picks.entryNote: SCHD provides 3.5%+ yield as an income alternative in a 4.25% Fed funds environment — the yield premium has compressed but the quality factor tilt makes this a portfolio anchor; no single-name concentration risk; confirmed holder across all prior portfolio dates
picks.exitTrigger: Fed delivers aggressive rate cuts (>100 bps in 12 months) making dividend yield less competitive vs. T-bills AND growth inflects back to risk-on, making quality/value tilts unnecessary; this is a low-probability scenario given current macro
picks.keyRisk: A sharp growth acceleration surprising to the upside causes a growth-over-value rotation that leaves SCHD significantly underperforming the broader market during the holding period

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the textbook all-weather compounder: $397.4B in cash and T-bills (up 6.5% from year-end) generating 5%+ risk-free income, zero holding-company debt, insurance float reinvestment benefiting from higher-for-longer rates, and a newly re-started buyback program under Greg Abel. In any equity correction scenario, Berkshire's war chest becomes optionality — Abel's Q1 2026 repositioning (tripling GOOGL, initiating DAL, exiting Visa/Mastercard) demonstrates a disciplined capital allocator comfortable acting decisively. BRK.B has near-zero correlation to AI hypergrowth tech, trades at ~1.4x book — undemanding — and functions as the portfolio's structural hedge against an AI multiple collapse scenario.
picks.catalyst: Greg Abel deploying part of the $397B war chest in a market correction would be a major catalyst; Q2 2026 earnings (July) showing insurance underwriting profit and investment income uplift from T-bill reinvestment at 5%+
picks.entryNote: BRK.B at ~1.4x book is historically undemanding — Buffett's buyback threshold was approximately 1.2x, so current levels provide reasonable upside with strong downside protection; Abel's buyback reactivation at $235M in Q1 is a signal of fair-to-cheap valuation; confirmed defensive anchor across all prior portfolio dates
picks.exitTrigger: Valuation expands above 2.0x book without commensurate earnings growth, or war chest deployment shifts to high-risk acquisitions outside Berkshire's circle of competence; insurance catastrophe losses exceeding $20B in a single year
picks.keyRisk: A prolonged risk-on environment where BRK.B significantly underperforms the S&P 500 as the cash hoard is seen as a drag rather than an asset — opportunity cost risk in a ripping bull market

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides duration exposure as a tail hedge against the single most underpriced scenario in current markets: a sharp growth slowdown that forces the Fed to pivot from hold/hike toward cuts, causing a flight-to-quality Treasury rally. The 10-year at 4.52% (down from 4.55% after the CPI print) offers the highest entry yield in years, with core CPI at 0.2% m/m suggesting the non-energy inflation picture is more contained. IEF is not a primary return driver but provides crucial negative correlation to the equity book during risk-off events, particularly as the BOJ hike risk on June 16 (82% probability priced) could trigger yen carry unwind and US Treasury demand surge. Medium-sized position reflects the uncertain duration of the inflation overhang.
picks.catalyst: BOJ rate hike on June 16 triggering yen carry unwind and safe-haven Treasury buying; any negative surprise in June non-farm payrolls or growth data shifting Fed narrative toward cuts; geopolitical escalation risk-off flight to quality
picks.catalystWindow: immediate — June 16 BOJ meeting is the most critical near-term catalyst
picks.entryNote: 10-year at 4.52% is the highest sustained yield level in over a decade — locking in income at these levels while having optionality on price appreciation if growth disappoints; the 2-year yield at 4.11% (below the 10-year) suggests the curve is pricing some slowing; medium conviction reflects genuine Fed uncertainty
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks above 5.25% on sustained basis (real rate +1%) as Fed hikes 2+ times, making IEF a continuous mark-to-market headwind; or inflation falls rapidly below 2.5% making equity risk premium more attractive
picks.keyRisk: Fed delivers multiple rate hikes in H2 2026 pushing the long end sharply higher, generating meaningful mark-to-market losses on IEF that offset its hedging value

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO hit an all-time high on Wednesday (June 10) — rising more than 2% while the S&P 500 fell 1.62% and tech stocks plunged — providing real-time evidence of defensive rotation in action. As the definitive consumer staples compounder with global pricing power, a 3%+ dividend yield, and near-zero sensitivity to AI capex cycles, KO is the most direct expression of consumer staples defensive positioning. In the late-cycle, negative-real-wage-growth environment described in the macro briefing, brand-essential consumer staples attract capital flight from discretionary. Berkshire Hathaway maintains KO as its third-largest position at 11.56% of disclosed equity portfolio — the longest-held strategic position in the book.
picks.catalyst: Continued defensive rotation as the tech correction deepens; Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) showing pricing power durability and international volume recovery; any Fed hawkish surprise at June 16-17 meeting accelerates defensive bid
picks.catalystWindow: immediate — the defensive rotation trade is live as of Wednesday's session; earnings confirmation next 5-7 weeks
picks.entryNote: KO at all-time highs may seem a counterintuitive entry but the breakout on a down tape day confirms institutional buying; dividend yield ~3%+ is competitive income; P/E ~22x is at the lower end of historical premium range; Berkshire's long-term hold validates quality of the franchise; confirmed defensive anchor across all prior portfolio dates
picks.exitTrigger: Two consecutive quarters of volume decline in developed markets indicating brand erosion from health-conscious secular shift; or a sustained risk-on rally lasting 6+ months that makes defensives deeply unattractive on relative returns
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 drug adoption (Ozempic, Zepbound) meaningfully reducing sugar-beverage consumption among the previously heaviest users, compressing long-term volume growth in a secular, not cyclical, fashion

summary: The portfolio is constructed around a late-cycle stagflationary regime with a Cautiously Bullish stance — the S&P 500 is in a tech-led correction (-11% from June 2 peak in the tech sector) following the hot 4.2% May CPI print, Iran escalation pushing WTI to $90/barrel, and Goldman's Risk Appetite Indicator at the 99th percentile since 1991. The growth book expresses the two dominant structural themes — AI infrastructure (NVDA, AVGO, AMZN-AWS, ORCL at three distinct points in the AI value chain) and energy/defense inflation beneficiaries (XOM, RTX) — while adding diversified compounders in Financials (GS, SPGI), Communication Services (GOOGL at a discount to all mega-cap peers), and Healthcare (MRK as a quality defensive growth anchor). The defensive sleeve — GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO — provides ~30% of the book as a genuine macro hedge, critical given that KO hit an all-time high on a down tape day (June 10) confirming the defensive rotation thesis is live. Carry-overs: all ten growth names were in the prior 7-day positioning log (NVDA, GOOGL, AVBO, AMZN, ORCL, XOM, RTX, GS, SPGI, MRK) maintaining thesis continuity; all five defensives (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) are unchanged — no names were dropped because no thesis breakdowns occurred, consistent with the rule that name churn destroys returns. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI multiple collapse (tech sector -11% already from peak) compounded by Fed hike execution risk at the June 16-17 FOMC meeting; the defensive sleeve and the within-AI diversification (custom ASICs via AVBO, cloud via AMZN, database/OCI via ORCL, alongside NVDA chips) are designed to manage that concentration.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans six GICS sectors — Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, ORCL; max 3 per sector rule met), Communication Services (GOOGL), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Financials (GS, SPGI), and Health Care (MRK) — with NVDA/AVGO/AMZN sharing AI infrastructure secular exposure but at deliberately different points in the value chain (GPU silicon, custom ASIC, cloud hyperscaler) to reduce single-factor correlation; the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold inflation/geopolitical hedge (GLD), broad dividend-quality equity ETF (SCHD), fortress conglomerate/cash (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples brand compounder (KO).

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet has been the highest-conviction carry-over since May 27, and today's selloff — driven by semi-specific AVGO miss and hawkish NFP — does not impair the GOOGL thesis. Google Cloud grew +63% in Q1 2026 following +48% in Q4 2025, Search accelerated +19% YoY, and operating margins expanded as AI workloads scaled. Berkshire added 225% to its GOOGL stake in Q1 2026, making it their 5th largest position. Today's broad tape selloff is an entry improvement, not a thesis break.
picks.catalyst: June 10 May CPI print + Q2 earnings (late July) with Cloud and Search guidance
picks.entryNote: Trading at reasonable forward multiple vs. growth rate; Cloud reacceleration justifies premium. Today's tech-wide selloff improves risk/reward at current prices vs. 5-day highs.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or antitrust ruling mandating Search divestiture
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust forced restructuring of Search distribution agreements; AI Overviews cannibalizes ad clicks at scale

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the dominant AI infrastructure pick despite today's semi selloff (-4%+ intraday). The AVGO miss was company-specific to custom ASIC guidance — NVDA's Blackwell/Rubin roadmap is intact, with Jensen Huang indicating up to $1 trillion in purchase orders through 2027. Q1 FY2027 revenue of $81.6B beat by $2.7B with net income at $58.3B vs. $42.9B expected. KeyBanc reaffirmed NVDA as 'best AI chip play' in the sector on today's tape. Today's dip is an accumulation opportunity, not a thesis break.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (August) with Blackwell ramp confirmation and export-control-adjusted guidance
picks.entryNote: Down ~4% today from recent highs; still richly valued at ~35x forward earnings but supported by 100%+ YoY EPS growth. Today's dip toward 50dma creates better risk/reward than last week's highs.
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 68%, or loss of a major hyperscaler design win to AMD or custom silicon
picks.keyRisk: Export controls on H20/Blackwell further restricting China revenue, which still represents ~15% of total

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the cleanest multi-engine compounder in the universe: AWS reaccelerated to +28% YoY in Q1 (vs. +24% Q4 2025), Bedrock processed more tokens in Q1 2026 than all prior years combined, and Trainium custom silicon now runs at $20B+ annual revenue run-rate growing triple digits. Tepper nearly doubled his position to #1 holding, and Ackman added +1.84M shares making it his #2. Today's broad selloff is not an AWS-specific event — thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) confirming AWS margin expansion and AI inference monetization
picks.entryNote: Has outperformed S&P 500 YTD; today's -2% dip in line with market, not company-specific. AWS reacceleration visible in data makes current levels attractive on a 1-3yr basis.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or operating margin compression below 8% consolidated
picks.keyRisk: FCF compression from accelerating capex ($105B+ 2026 guidance) if cloud monetization lags spend

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: ORCL was added on June 3 and moved to rank #5 on June 4 as a differentiated AI cloud pick with lower direct exposure to the AVGO-specific narrative. Oracle Cloud Infrastructure is the fastest-growing major cloud at scale, winning multi-billion dollar AI training contracts from hyperscalers needing neutral third-party capacity. OCI's remaining performance obligations (RPO) backlog exceeded $130B last quarter, providing multi-year revenue visibility that is not AI-hype dependent. Today's selloff — driven by AVGO custom ASIC miss — does not touch Oracle's database/cloud SaaS franchise.
picks.catalyst: FQ4 2026 earnings (mid-June) with cloud RPO and new AI contract announcements
picks.entryNote: Pulls back with broader tech today despite no company-specific negative; trades at premium to historical multiple but justified by cloud RPO backlog growth rate. Near-term earnings are a live catalyst.
picks.exitTrigger: Cloud RPO backlog growth stalls below 20% YoY, or a major hyperscaler customer cancels infrastructure contracts
picks.keyRisk: AWS, Azure, and GCP could undercut OCI on pricing to win back AI workloads previously ceded to neutral cloud

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta posted Q1 2026 EPS of $7.31 vs. $6.79 estimated (7.7% beat), with net income climbing to $26.8B from $16.6B a year earlier, and ad revenue accelerating +33% YoY — the fastest in years — powered by AI-optimized targeting. Today's additional pressure from a secondary offering is a short-term technical overhang, not a fundamental thesis break. Meta's AI agent monetization and custom silicon buildout (1GW with Broadcom) give it a durable earnings growth runway. Free cash flow generation more than justifies capex, unlike pure-infrastructure peers.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your senior macro strategy briefing for **Friday, June 5, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO REGIME DASHBOARD — US EQUITIES | June 5, 2026

---

### THE DATA TAPE

**FED POLICY**

The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75%
, now 
the third consecutive meeting where the committee chose to stand pat – following three consecutive cuts last year.
 The April 29 meeting was unusually contentious: 
the FOMC split along 8–4 lines, with officials expressing different reasons for their vote
 — 
the last time four members dissented was October 1992.
 On the dot plot, 
the median of the March 2026 Fed dot plot projections sees the fed funds rate falling just 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027,
 implying a terminal rate around 3.25%. 
Notably, the "longer run" anchor has shifted upward to 3.0%, signaling a collective belief that the neutral rate is higher than previously estimated.
 
Fed officials foresee the funds rate settling down to its expected neutral level around 3.1%.
 Market-implied path: 
markets are pricing in no changes the rest of this year and well into 2027,
 with 
an ~85% chance of a quarter-point rate *hike* by year-end, up from 60% a week ago,
 as energy-driven inflation reaccelerates. The real policy rate (Fed funds midpoint 3.625% vs. core PCE ~3.3%) is barely positive — policy is only modestly restrictive relative to neutral at ~3.0–3.1%.

**YIELD CURVE**

As of June 4, 2026, the US Treasury yield curve is upward-sloping: the 2-Year yields 4.05%, the 10-Year 4.48%, and the 30-Year 4.98%.
 
The 10Y vs. 2Y spread (2s10s) is +42 bps; the 10Y vs. 3M spread (3m10y) is +76 bps.
 Crucially, 
the 10-year yield hovered around 4.48% after climbing on stronger-than-expected labor market data, reinforcing expectations of tighter Fed policy.
 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support higher long-maturity yields, consistent with upward pressure on long-end benchmarks.
 Term premium is rising — structurally driven by persistent inflation uncertainty and heavy sovereign supply.

**INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING**

The annual inflation rate accelerated to 3.8% in April 2026, the highest since May 2023, compared to 3.3% in March — above forecasts of 3.7% — as the oil shock triggered by the war with Iran continues to push prices higher.
 
Energy costs jumped 17.9%, the steepest annual increase since September 2022, with gasoline up 28.4% and fuel oil up 54.3%.
 
Core CPI edged higher to 2.8% year-on-year, the highest since September, from 2.6% in March.
 On PCE: 
the PCE price index rose 0.4% in April (3.8% y/y), marking the third consecutive month of rapid inflation; the core PCE rose 0.2% (3.3% y/y).
 The March SEP projected 
a 30-bps increase in yearend 2026 PCE inflation (now 2.7%).
 
The softer-than-expected 0.24% m/m gain in core PCE in April buys the Fed a bit of time as it waits to see how higher oil prices will feed through to broader inflation pressures.
 Trajectory: **re-accelerating**, energy-led with secondary shelter stickiness.

**GROWTH**

Per the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, real GDP growth is currently tracking at 3% (quarter-over-quarter annualized basis) in Q2.
 However, 
Q1 GDP growth was revised down to 1.6% SAAR from 2.0% in the advance estimate, with the downward revision almost entirely due to a larger inventory drawdown.
 On labor: 
NFP is expected to rise ~85K in May (today's release), slowing from the 115K gain in April; the unemployment rate is forecast to hold at 4.3%.
 The three-month payroll run-rate is modest (~125–160K/month based on March's +178K and April's +115K). 
The third consecutive consensus-beating gain in nonfarm payrolls in May, if confirmed, should further reduce FOMC concern about downside labor market risks.
 On ISMs: 
Manufacturing PMI registered 54% in May, its highest reading since May 2022.
 
Among surveyed manufacturers, the Iran war was mentioned in 42% of comments and tariffs in 18%; 57% cited pricing volatility as an issue.
 Services: 
ISM Services PMI increased to 54.5 in May from 53.6 in April, the strongest gain in three months.
 
Employment contracted for a third consecutive month (47.9), while price pressures intensified to the highest since August 2022 (71.3) — diesel, gasoline, and oil again the primary culprits.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

As of early 2026, broad investment-grade OAS is roughly ~80 bps.
 HY OAS data from FRED is current but specific levels were unavailable in today's pull — directionally, 
the HY-IG spread is trending down and SPX is rising, indicative of near-term risk-on sentiment.
 DXY: 
the US Dollar Index (DXY) fell toward 99.18 before recovering to ~99.45 on Thursday, as investors digested Fed commentary ahead of today's NFP release.
 Financial conditions are loose overall — the curve is positively sloped, spreads are tight, and dollar weakness has provided an additional easing impulse. 
Treasury yields have found support from escalating Middle East tensions, which keep oil prices elevated and add to inflationary pressures.


**EARNINGS BACKDROP**

The S&P 500 climbed to another record high recently, closing at 7,580.
 
The index extended its advance for a ninth consecutive week, rising more than 5% in May.
 
Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February 2026,
 driven by AI infrastructure demand. 
The forward P/E on the S&P 500 stands at approximately 25.9x (Dec 2026 basis)
 — materially above the 10-year average of ~18–19x. 
The four largest hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta — collectively spent north of $400 billion in capex in 2025, nearly 70% more than in 2024.
 The spread between AI capex intensity and near-term earnings delivery is a latent valuation risk.

**GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE**

A string of resilient US economic indicators has reinforced hawkish Fed expectations, while peace negotiations between the US and Iran are reportedly nearing their final stage — but Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said no meaningful progress had been made, and Iran-backed Hezbollah rejected a US-mediated ceasefire proposal, keeping geopolitical risks elevated.
 
Policymakers face an economic climate where inflation has held well above the Fed's 2% target, as tariffs and soaring energy prices complicate policy.
 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17, 2026.


---

## 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**LATE-CYCLE.** The regime exhibits the classic late-cycle signature across all major pillars: 
positive but compressed spreads (2s10s +42 bps, 3m10y +76 bps) consistent with a cautious growth outlook and persistent inflation pressures that keep the Fed on hold.
 Nominal growth is still positive — GDPNow tracking +3% in Q2 — but Q1 was revised down to a soft 1.6%, labor is hiring at a decelerating pace, and 
services employment has now contracted for three consecutive months.
 Meanwhile, inflation is re-accelerating (CPI 3.8%, PCE 3.8% y/y) in a supply-shock context, leaving the Fed frozen on the sideline with a real policy rate barely above zero. This is textbook late-cycle: growth still above trend but losing momentum, inflation uncomfortably sticky/rising, policy rates on hold with a hawkish drift, term premium expanding, and equity markets priced for perfection at ~26x forward earnings.

---

## 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's Working:** Energy and defense are natural beneficiaries of the Iran war premium — 
S&P 500 forward EPS revisions are still trending higher even since the start of the Iran conflict, with energy estimates getting a boost.
 
Manufacturing at a 4-year high ISM (54%) with 19 straight months of broad expansion
 lifts Industrials and Materials. 
Technology estimates have been revised sharply higher,
 supported by the AI capex super-cycle; large-cap Tech/Semis (particularly AI infrastructure names like Oracle and Micron) are carrying the index to record highs. Quality/Profitability factors screen well in late cycle — companies with pricing power and low variable cost exposure to energy. Short-duration equities (Financials with steeper curve; Energy) and real assets outperform. **What's Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors are under pressure: 
inflation elevated, energy prices rising
 — this squeezes Consumer Discretionary margins and Housing. 
The prices-paid index at multi-year highs signals mounting supply-chain stress and margin pressure, likely restraining near-term investment and production for mid-cap Industrials.
 
Services employment is contracting for a third consecutive month
 — a leading stress indicator for labor-intensive sectors (Retail, Healthcare Services). Long-duration growth/SMID cap are most vulnerable to any further yield backup. The 26x forward P/E leaves essentially zero margin of safety for negative earnings surprises.

---

## 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH

**1. Today's May Nonfarm Payrolls (June 5) + June CPI (June 10):** 
Investors are awaiting today's nonfarm payrolls report for further clues on labor market strength
 — consensus expects ~85K but 
the third consecutive consensus-beating print
 would further close the door on any 2026 rate cut and 
markets are already pricing an 85% chance of a *hike* by year-end.
 June 10 CPI (May data) is equally pivotal: 
the Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled to be released on June 10 at 8:30 a.m.
 A third consecutive hot print would materially reprice the entire rates and equity complex. **2. June 16–17 FOMC Meeting + New Dot Plot:** 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17.
 With the April vote fracturing 8–4 and 
a majority of participants flagging that policy firming could become appropriate if inflation continues to run persistently above 2%,
 this meeting carries a live risk of a hawkish pivot in language or even a surprise hike — an event that would severely stress equities at current valuations. **3. Middle East Ceasefire/Escalation Binary:** 
Trump says peace talks are nearing their final stage, but Iran's foreign minister reports no meaningful progress, and Hezbollah has rejected a US-mediated Lebanon ceasefire,
 keeping the oil shock live. A genuine ceasefire would deliver a sharp disinflationary impulse — unlocking Fed cuts, compressing yields, and rotating the tape from Energy/Defense into rate-sensitive laggards. Escalation does the opposite and forces the Fed's hand higher. This is the single highest-optionality event on the 1–6 month horizon.

### 2. TSLA — Tesla
**Structural demand failure + brand impairment + valuation still stretched for a car company**


FY2025 revenue came in at $94.8 billion, down 3% year-over-year — the first annual revenue decline in Tesla's history as a public company.
 The delivery trajectory is deteriorating: 
the company peaked at 1.81 million deliveries in 2023, declined to 1.79 million in 2024, and fell further to 1.636 million in 2025.
 In Q1 2026, 
Tesla produced 50,363 more vehicles than it delivered, with almost all of that excess in the Model 3/Y category where production of 394,611 outpaced deliveries of 341,893 by nearly 53,000 units
 — a clear demand-side problem, not a supply one. The energy storage "bright spot" also cracked: 
Tesla deployed just 8.8 GWh of energy storage in Q1 2026 — a 38% drop from Q4 2025's 14.2 GWh — which removes the one reliable growth story Tesla had.
 On brand: 
the past year has been a challenge as rivals offer higher-tech but lower-cost models, and Tesla faces ongoing consumer backlash in response to CEO Elon Musk's work with the Trump administration and his incendiary political rhetoric.
 Regulatory credit revenue — a near-100% margin stream — 
declined sharply, with Tesla generating about $380 million in Q1 2026, down from $542 million in Q4 2025, a drop of nearly 30% in just one quarter.
 TSLA is still trading like a tech/AI company, not an automaker posting sequential delivery declines.

---

### 3. SNOW — Snowflake
**Consumption model exposure + hyperscaler disintermediation risk + elevated valuation**


After a rough 2024, Snowflake's product revenue reaccelerated to 29% growth, and its Cortex AI layer and consumption-based model position it as the data backbone for enterprise AI.
 But the consumption model is a double-edged sword: when enterprise IT budgets tighten or when workloads migrate to in-house model infrastructure, revenue growth can decelerate sharply and immediately. The risk is structural: AWS, Azure, and GCP are all building competitive data analytics and AI inference layers that directly undercut Snowflake's use case. Snowflake still trades at a significant revenue multiple, pricing in multi-year hypergrowth — any quarter where NRR (net revenue retention) dips below 120% or where large customer churn appears could trigger a **-30%+ single-quarter re-rating**. 
Main market crash risks in 2026 include an AI/tech valuation reset
 — and Snowflake is among the most exposed pure-play names in the event of such a reset.

**Generative AI existential threat to core creative tools + antitrust overhang + margin pressure**

Adobe's core risk is existential disruption: every frontier AI lab (OpenAI, Midjourney, Runway, Sora, Stable Diffusion) is commoditizing image and video generation at zero marginal cost, directly attacking Photoshop, Premiere, and Illustrator. Adobe's $20B+ attempted acquisition of Figma was blocked by regulators, signaling that its path to growth via M&A is constrained. 
Adobe pays no dividends but delivers strong free cash flow with moderate buybacks; Adobe's distribution strategy is backed by superior margins and higher FCF per share.
 However, the competitive moat from its subscription lock-in is being tested: Creative Cloud's pricing power is at risk if creative professionals migrate to AI-native alternatives at a fraction of the cost. 
Adobe faces regulatory scrutiny mainly around data privacy and antitrust in digital advertising markets.
 If ARR growth decelerates from the mid-teens to high single digits, a multiple compression from ~25x forward earnings toward 18x would imply **-25–30% downside**.

## 3. 🌩️ MACRO TAIL RISKS — Top 3 (12-Month Horizon)

### TAIL RISK #1: Stagflation Trap — Fed Policy Paralysis
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**


If the labor market continues to weaken and inflation remains above the Fed's 2% target as tariffs trickle down to consumers, the big concern becomes stagflation — a rare condition where inflation and unemployment both run high.
 
J.P. Morgan notes that sticky inflation remains a global theme, limiting the ability of central banks to lower interest rates.
 The trigger: a hot CPI print (inflation already noted to have topped 4% per one source) coinciding with a negative jobs report forces the Fed to hold rates even as growth slows. 
US inflation has already topped 4% for the first time in three years as oil prices jumped.
 **Sectors most hurt**: rate-sensitive growth (SNOW, PLTR, high-P/E SaaS), long-duration bonds (TLT), REITs (PLD, EQIX), consumer discretionary (TGT, SBUX). **Sectors that survive or benefit**: energy (XOM, COP), gold (GLD), quality dividend (SCHD, VYM), healthcare (LLY, ABBV).

---

### TAIL RISK #2: AI Capex ROI Disappointment / Tech Multiple Collapse
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**


The risk that massive AI spending commitments — in the tens and hundreds of billions — don't come through should not be dismissed, particularly given some is being financed with debt, and OpenAI does not yet have the business model to support its planned investment.
 The consensus 2026 hyperscaler capex of ~$520B (up ~30% YoY) prices in flawless AI monetization. 
A downside scenario assumes AI investment gets overdone, leading to a sharp pullback in business spending in 2027 as companies reassess potential demand for related products, with real business investment expected to decline by 3.2% in 2027.
 **Trigger**: two consecutive quarters of hyperscaler capex guidance cuts, or a major cloud provider (Amazon, Microsoft, Google) announcing AI product revenue materially below infrastructure cost. 
A potential reset in AI expectations could act as an additional drag on equity markets, especially since AI names carry significant weight in major indices, amplifying market volatility and encouraging further de-risking.
 **Sectors most hurt**: NVDA, AMD, AVGO, MRVL, ARM (infrastructure), PLTR, SNOW, NET, ZS (SaaS beneficiaries), EQIX, AMT (data center REITs). S&P 500 index-level impact: -15% to -25% given concentration in AI-exposed mega caps.

---

### TAIL RISK #3: Middle East Escalation / Oil Supply Shock
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**


After withstanding higher trade barriers last year, global activity now faces a major test from the outbreak of war in the Middle East; assuming
